name: Hubal
description: Hubal was a prominent deity worshipped in pre-Islamic Arabia, particularly in the region of Mecca. He was considered one of the chief gods of the Kaaba, where his idol, reportedly made of carnelian, was housed. Historical accounts suggest that Hubal was associated with divination and prophecy, and his idol was often consulted using arrows for guidance and decision-making. Some scholars believe Hubal was linked to weather phenomena and fertility, although definitive evidence remains scarce. He was a significant figure in the polytheistic pantheon of the time, representing power and influence before the rise of Islam. Hubal's worship was deeply intertwined with the tribal structure and social customs of pre-Islamic Arabian society. The conquest of Mecca by Muhammad and the subsequent destruction of idols in the Kaaba marked the end of Hubal's prominence as a religious figure.
subtitle: Chief god of the Kaaba.

keyMyths.title: Hubal's Idol
keyMyths.description: Hubal's idol, made of carnelian, was housed inside the Kaaba in Mecca. It was used for divination through arrows before making important decisions.

keyMyths.title: Consultation via Arrows
keyMyths.description: The arrows were cast before the idol to seek guidance on matters of marriage, lineage, and warfare. The results were interpreted by priests serving the temple.

keyMyths.title: Hubal and the Quraish
keyMyths.description: The Quraish tribe of Mecca heavily revered Hubal, considering him a protector and guide. His worship was central to their religious and social practices.

sources.author: Ibn al-Kalbi
sources.description: A detailed account of pre-Islamic Arabian deities and their worship.
sources.text: A detailed account of pre-Islamic Arabian deities and their worship.
